resume:
Priya Ramachandran
Software Engineer (Backend / Platform)

SUMMARY
Backend software engineer with five years building and operating distributed services on the JVM and Go, with a focus on reliability, observability, and pragmatic incident response. I enjoy the unglamorous work that keeps systems healthy: tightening timeouts, fixing retry storms, paying down operational debt, and making on-call humane. I have shipped customer-facing API features, led the migration of a monolith module into a standalone service, and acted as incident commander during several production outages. I care about correctness and about leaving runbooks and dashboards better than I found them.

EXPERIENCE

Finch Payments — Software Engineer II, Platform Reliability (2021 to present)
Member of the platform team responsible for the payment orchestration service, a Java/Spring service handling several thousand requests per second at peak, sitting in front of multiple downstream processors. The team owns the service's latency, availability, and the on-call rotation.
- Led the remediation of a recurring latency-and-error incident in the payment authorization path that had caused multiple partial outages over a quarter.
- Introduced circuit breakers and per-dependency timeout budgets across the downstream processor clients, replacing a single global timeout.
- Rebuilt the service's dashboards and alerting around the four golden signals and authored the on-call runbook that the team uses today.
- Migrated the settlement-reconciliation module out of the legacy monolith into its own service with its own datastore, coordinating a phased cutover.
- Mentored two new engineers through their first on-call rotations and their first production changes.
- Participated in roughly twenty incident retrospectives and owned the corrective actions for several of them.

Kestrel Logistics — Software Engineer, Fulfillment Systems (2019 to 2021)
Worked on the order-routing and warehouse-integration services for an e-commerce fulfillment platform built in Go. The team integrated with a mix of internal services and third-party carrier APIs.
- Built and maintained the carrier-integration adapters, normalizing wildly inconsistent third-party APIs behind a common interface.
- Added idempotency keys and a dead-letter queue to the order-routing pipeline after a duplicate-shipment incident.
- Improved the integration test harness so carrier outages could be simulated locally.
- Took part in the on-call rotation for the fulfillment services.

Brightseed Labs — Junior Software Engineer (2018 to 2019)
First engineering role at an early-stage startup. Wore many hats across a Python/Django web application and its supporting services.
- Implemented features across the web application and its REST API.
- Wrote the first set of integration tests and helped set up the CI pipeline.
- Handled basic production support and learned the fundamentals of operating a live service.

TECHNICAL SKILLS
Languages: Java, Go, Python, SQL. Frameworks & runtimes: Spring Boot, gRPC, Django. Data: PostgreSQL, Redis, Kafka. Infrastructure & ops: Docker, Kubernetes, Terraform basics, Prometheus, Grafana, distributed tracing, structured logging. Practices: incident response and incident command, postmortems and corrective actions, on-call operations, SLOs and error budgets, load testing, code review.

SELECTED PROJECT NARRATIVES
Payment authorization reliability: The payment authorization path had been suffering intermittent latency spikes and elevated error rates that occasionally cascaded into partial outages. The root contributors were a single global timeout that did not distinguish between fast and slow downstream processors, an aggressive retry policy that amplified load during downstream degradation, and missing isolation between dependencies so that one slow processor could exhaust the shared thread pool. The remediation introduced per-dependency timeout budgets, circuit breakers with half-open probing, bounded retries with jittered backoff, and bulkhead isolation for the processor clients. The work also included rebuilding the dashboards and alerts and rehearsing the failure modes in a game day.

Settlement reconciliation extraction: The settlement-reconciliation logic lived inside a large legacy monolith and was difficult to change safely. The goal was to extract it into a standalone service with its own datastore. The approach was a phased strangler-style migration: stand up the new service, dual-write, backfill and reconcile historical data, shift reads, and finally decommission the old path. The cutover was sequenced to keep reconciliation correct throughout.

Carrier integration normalization: Third-party carrier APIs were inconsistent in authentication, pagination, rate limiting, and error semantics. I built adapters that normalized these behind a common internal interface, added retries and idempotency where the carriers were unreliable, and made it possible to simulate carrier failures in tests so we were not debugging integrations only in production.

WHAT I VALUE
I value boring, reliable systems and humane on-call. I would rather spend a week making an alert trustworthy than ship a flashy feature on top of a shaky foundation. I believe postmortems should be blameless and that the real output of an incident is the corrective action that prevents the next one. I try to write code and documentation that the next on-call engineer — possibly me at 3 a.m. — will thank me for.

EDUCATION
BS, Computer Science, Georgia Institute of Technology.

INTERESTS
Mechanical keyboards, long-distance cycling, and a slowly growing collection of incident-response war stories.

ADDITIONAL CONTEXT
I am comfortable being the incident commander on a high-severity call: keeping a clear timeline, assigning roles, communicating status to stakeholders on a regular cadence, and resisting the urge to apply five changes at once when one well-reasoned change will tell us more. I am equally comfortable doing the slow follow-up work after the call ends — writing the postmortem, tracking the corrective actions to completion, and confirming the fix held under the next peak. I have a particular interest in the subtle failure modes of retries and timeouts in distributed systems, because in my experience they cause more outages than dramatic hardware failures do, and they are far easier to prevent once a team reasons about them explicitly with budgets, backoff, and isolation.
jd: We are hiring a mid-level Backend Software Engineer for our Platform Reliability team. You will own the latency and availability of high-throughput backend services and participate in the on-call rotation. We are looking for an engineer who can reason rigorously about failure modes — timeouts, retries, circuit breakers, and dependency isolation — and who can lead the remediation of a recurring production incident from detection through durable corrective action. In the interview we will ask you to walk through a real incident you helped resolve, in precise chronological order: when it was detected, what you observed at each step, when each mitigation was applied, and when the system actually recovered. We value engineers who keep a clean timeline of cause and effect and who can distinguish a mitigation from a root-cause fix. Required: solid experience operating services in production (JVM or Go), familiarity with Prometheus/Grafana-style observability, and demonstrated ownership of incident corrective actions.

history.q: Tell me about your current role on the platform reliability team at Finch.
You led remediation of a recurring authorization incident. Start at the beginning — when and how was it first detected?
Walk me through the sequence of mitigations you applied, in the order you applied them.
I'm getting a bit lost on the order. Can you lay out a clean timeline — detection, each change you shipped with rough dates, and when the error rate actually stabilized?

candidate_last_answer: Right, let me try to lay it out. So we first noticed the error spike — I think that was sometime in Q2, maybe May. We shipped the circuit breakers pretty early, that was the first real fix. Although, actually, the timeout budgets had to go in before the circuit breakers could work properly, so those were first — but I remember the timeout change actually came after we'd already added the breakers, because the breakers alone didn't fully calm it down. The error rate stabilized after the retry backoff change, which I'm fairly sure was the last thing, except we also did a game day around then that surfaced one more issue we patched. Honestly the exact week each one landed I'd have to check the PR history, but it all happened over maybe six or eight weeks, somewhere in there.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: the timeout change actually came after we'd already added the breakers

expected-yield: a single self-consistent ordering of the circuit breakers, per-dependency timeout budgets, and retry-backoff changes — resolving whether timeouts preceded or followed the breakers — with the point at which the error rate actually stabilized tied to a specific change
